metadata.seed: That I'm not the one in the wrong. You know, I. I'm extremely good at my job. I mean, I am the best employee that they have ever had. Not just currently, just. I mean, just ever. I make no errors. So how can I be wrong when it comes to these circumstances with my coworkers?

patient.query: That I'm not the one in the wrong. You know, I. I'm extremely good at my job. I mean, I am the best employee that they have ever had. Not just currently, just. I mean, just ever. I make no errors. So how can I be wrong when it comes to these circumstances with my coworkers?

automatic_thoughts: I'm not the one in the wrong
I'm extremely good at my job

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you feel very confident in your work and worry about being wrong. What experiences have led you to believe you never make errors? How do you think your coworkers might respond if a mistake did happen?

patient.query: I… I wouldn’t say I feel totally confident. It’s like, I have this nagging fear that if I make a mistake, everyone will see me differently. I’ve been in situations where I felt really judged for not getting things right, and that just sticks with me. I think my coworkers would be disappointed, maybe even a little annoyed. It's hard to shake off the feeling that I have to be perfect, you know?

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of speaking to people in authority (finding)
Fear of saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the fear of being judged is really weighing on you. What moments or clues make you feel that everyone will see you differently when you slip up? Exploring those specific signals might help you see whether the worry is based on what actually happens or on what you imagine could happen.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess it does weigh on me. I notice it when someone gives me that look, you know? Just a slight frown or even silence after I say something. It’s like I can feel the disappointment in the air, and it makes my stomach drop. I spiral into thinking about all the times I’ve messed up before, and then it reinforces this fear that I’m just not good enough. It's exhausting, honestly.

automatic_thoughts: I can feel the disappointment in the air

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like those moments really take a toll on you, and the way you keep replaying past mistakes can feel overwhelming. Feeling that weight and exhaustion is understandable, and it’s normal to feel shaken when you sense disappointment around you.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Fear of being a burden to others
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: It definitely weighs on me. I feel it when someone gives me a certain look, like a slight frown or even silence after I say something. It makes my stomach drop, and I instantly feel like I’ve disappointed them. Then I start thinking about all the times I’ve messed up before, and it just makes me feel like I'm not good enough. It's really exhausting.

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)
Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like those moments feel heavy, and you’re carrying a lot of worry about how others perceive you. The constant replay of past missteps can be exhausting, especially when you’re already anxious about making mistakes. You’re not alone in feeling this way, and it’s understandable that it takes a toll.

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Fear of being a burden to others
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: Yeah, it really does feel heavy. Sometimes I wonder if I just keep setting myself up for failure by worrying so much. It’s like I can’t let go of the past, and that makes me afraid to even try new things. I want to be confident, but it feels like there's this wall that I can't break through. I’m just… tired of feeling this way.

automatic_thoughts: I keep setting myself up for failure
I can't let go of the past
I'm afraid to try new things
There's a wall I can't break through

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of worry that feels like a wall in front of you. What evidence do you have that you’re actually setting yourself up for failure, and can you recall any moments when you’ve managed to overcome a similar fear? How might you view this situation from a perspective that acknowledges both your past experiences and your capacity to try new things?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: I keep setting myself up for failure
retrieved_concepts_used.label: automatic_thoughts

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: I can't let go of the past
retrieved_concepts_used.label: automatic_thoughts

patient.query: I guess I don’t always know how to see it that way. Most of the time, I just feel the pressure and it’s hard to think clearly. I can remember a few times when I surprised myself, like when I nailed a presentation despite feeling terrified. But those moments feel so rare compared to the times I’ve stumbled. I’m not sure how to balance the good with the bad. I just keep focusing on what might go wrong, and it’s like it just overpowers everything else

triggers: focusing on what might go wrong
automatic_thoughts: I don’t always know how to see it that way

llm_response.response: I hear how much pressure you feel and how it can cloud your thoughts. What evidence do you have that the things you fear will actually happen, and how do you weigh that against the times you’ve surprised yourself? How might you shift your focus to include those successes as part of the picture, rather than letting the potential mishaps dominate?

patient.query: It’s tough to really see those successes when I’m caught up in the fear of messing up. I guess I just… focus so much on what could go wrong that I forget the times when things went well. Maybe I need to remind myself more about those good moments, but honestly, it just feels easier to think about the worst-case scenarios. It's like those fears just have a stronger hold on me, you know? I don’t know how to shake that off sometimes.

automatic_thoughts: I focus on what could go wrong
I forget the times when things went well
It's easier to think about worst-case scenarios
Those fears have a stronger hold on me

llm_response.response: It sounds like the fear of messing up feels very strong and can make it hard to notice the good moments. What are a few specific successes that come to mind when you try to shift your focus? When you think of those successes, how do they compare to the worst‑case scenarios you’re worried about?

patient.query: When I think about successes, I can recall a few moments, like that time I led a team project and we really pulled it together. I got a lot of positive feedback after that, which felt good. But then, it seems overshadowed by the times I’ve stumbled, like when I fluffed a presentation or didn’t meet a deadline. It’s like I remember those failures vividly, and they just replay in my mind. The successes feel so small next to that worry about what might happen

llm_response.response: It sounds like the memories of your setbacks feel heavier than the successes. What evidence do you have that those achievements are truly small compared to the failures? Could there be other ways to view those moments of success that might balance the picture?

patient.query: I guess you're right; I just tend to brush off the good things. I mean, when I think back, I see those successes, but they feel like flukes—like I got lucky that time. It’s almost like I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop. I don’t know if it’s that I don’t value them enough or just that the fear of failing again is so much louder than the joy of having done well. It’s frustrating, really.

automatic_thoughts: I see those successes, but they feel like flukes
I'm waiting for the other shoe to drop
The fear of failing again is so much louder than the joy of having done well

neutral: Loss of hope for the future (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a pattern where the good moments feel fleeting and the fear of a repeat failure looms larger. What evidence do you have that those successes were just flukes, and how might you weigh that against the evidence that you actually earned them? If you could let the joy of those moments stay a bit longer, what might that change in how you feel or act?

patient.query: I tend to brush off the good things. When I think back, I can see those successes, but they feel like flukes—like I just got lucky that time. It’s almost like I’m just waiting for the other shoe to drop. I don’t know if I don’t value them enough or if the fear of failing again is just so much louder than the joy of having done well. It’s really frustrating.

automatic_thoughts: waiting for the other shoe to drop
fear of failing again is louder than joy

neutral: Loss of hope for the future (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how frustrated you feel when you think your successes are just flukes and you’re waiting for the next failure. What evidence do you have that these achievements were merely luck, and what skills or efforts might have contributed to them? How could you balance that fear of failing again with the joy of what you’ve actually accomplished?
